title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of antibiotics. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Miracle Mold: The Story of Penicillin
content.text: Before the mid-20th century, a simple scratch or a minor infection could be a death sentence. Hospitals were filled with patients suffering from septicemia, pneumonia, and gangrene, for which doctors had no effective treatment other than amputation or prayer. The world was on the brink of a medical revolution, but it arrived in the most unexpected way—through a forgotten petri dish. ||(41)|| Returning from a vacation in 1928, Fleming noticed that a common mold, *Penicillium notatum*, had contaminated one of his cultures of staphylococcus bacteria. To his astonishment, the bacteria immediately surrounding the mold had been destroyed. He had discovered the first antibiotic, a substance that could kill pathogens without harming human cells. ||(42)|| Despite the brilliance of the discovery, Fleming lacked the chemical expertise to isolate and mass-produce the active ingredient. For over a decade, penicillin remained a laboratory curiosity, a 'wonder drug' that could not be used because there wasn't enough of it to treat a single human patient. ||(43)|| Working at Oxford University, they managed to purify the substance into a stable powder. However, with World War II raging and British factories under constant bombardment, they realized that they could not produce the drug in the quantities needed for the battlefield. They turned to the United States for help. ||(44)|| This industrial effort was successful beyond all expectations. By D-Day in 1944, enough penicillin had been produced to treat every wounded Allied soldier. The drug didn't just save lives; it changed the outcome of the war, drastically reducing the mortality rate from infected wounds. ||(45)|| However, the victory was not absolute. Even in the early days of its use, Fleming himself warned that the overuse of penicillin would lead to the evolution of resistant 'superbugs.' If the drug was used too frequently or in doses that were too small, the bacteria would learn to survive the attack and pass that resistance to the next generation. ||(46)|| Today, we find ourselves in a race against time. We are developing new classes of antibiotics, but the bacteria are evolving faster than our labs can keep up. The 'miracle' of the 20th century is under threat, reminding us that in the world of biology, no victory is ever final.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] this breakthrough was the result [/41] of a fortunate accident in the laboratory of Alexander Fleming. A man known for his somewhat untidy work habits, Fleming had left a stack of plates near an open window before leaving for the weekend.
paragraphs.B: This [42] 'mold juice,' as he initially called it [/42], was incredibly potent. Fleming published his findings in an academic journal, but the scientific community was largely indifferent, as many believed that such a volatile substance could never be turned into a practical medicine.
paragraphs.C: It wasn't until [43] Howard Florey and Ernst Chain took up the challenge [/43] in the late 1930s that the situation changed. They were determined to turn Fleming's observation into a medical reality, even if it meant using improvised equipment like milk churns and bathtubs.
paragraphs.D: In [44] Peoria, Illinois, scientists discovered [/44] that by growing the mold in a 'soup' of corn steep liquor, they could increase the yield exponentially. This American innovation turned a painstaking laboratory process into a massive industrial operation.
paragraphs.F: Following [45] this unprecedented success [/45], antibiotics were hailed as the end of infectious disease. Doctors began prescribing them for everything from the common cold to minor skin rashes, ignoring the subtle signs that the bacteria were already beginning to adapt.
paragraphs.E: This [46] prophetic warning has now become a reality [/46]. Multi-drug-resistant infections are becoming increasingly common in hospitals worldwide, forcing us to rethink our entire approach to hygiene and the pharmaceutical industry.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] people mistakenly believe [/47] that antibiotics are effective against viruses like the flu. In reality, these drugs only target the cell walls or metabolic processes of bacteria, leaving viral structures completely untouched, which contributes to the problem of misuse.

questions.explanation: A identifies the 'fortunate accident' in Alexander Fleming's lab, which explains the 'forgotten petri dish' mentioned in the opening paragraph.
B refers to Fleming's initial name for the substance ('mold juice') and explains why the scientific community was initially 'indifferent' to his discovery.
C introduces Howard Florey and Ernst Chain, who took up the challenge of purifying the drug as mentioned in the text following the gap.
D describes the 'American innovation' in Peoria that allowed for 'massive industrial operation', linking to the help from the US mentioned before.
F refers to the 'unprecedented success' of penicillin during the war and the subsequent 'over-prescription' that led to the problems described next.
E links back to Fleming's 'prophetic warning' about superbugs and explains that it has now become a 'reality' in modern hospitals.
